question: A Claude Code agent is running in a CI/CD pipeline reviewing pull requests. After submitting the review, the pipeline hangs indefinitely. What is the most likely cause?
options: The agent's system prompt is too long and exceeds the context window
Claude Code was launched without the -p / --print flag, causing it to wait for interactive input
The tool_choice parameter was set to 'auto' instead of 'any'
The CLAUDE.md file was not committed to the repository
explanation: Without the -p (--print) flag, Claude Code runs in interactive mode and waits for keyboard input. In a CI/CD pipeline there is no keyboard, so the pipeline hangs indefinitely. The -p flag is required for all non-interactive/headless usage.
whyWrong: Context window overflow causes an API error, not a hang — the pipeline would fail with an error message
This is the correct answer
tool_choice affects whether Claude calls a tool or returns text — it does not cause a pipeline hang
Missing CLAUDE.md causes Claude to operate without project conventions — it does not cause a hang

question: A development team wants to ensure that all code generated by Claude Code always follows the repository pattern for database access. A senior engineer proposes adding this rule to the project CLAUDE.md. A junior engineer argues a PreToolUse hook that validates generated code before writing is better. Which is architecturally correct and why?
options: CLAUDE.md is better because it applies to every session automatically without additional infrastructure
The PreToolUse hook is better because it provides deterministic enforcement — CLAUDE.md guidance is probabilistic
Both are equivalent — either approach provides the same level of compliance assurance
CLAUDE.md is better because hooks add latency to every file write operation
explanation: CLAUDE.md provides probabilistic guidance — Claude will follow it most of the time, but LLMs have a non-zero failure rate. A PreToolUse hook provides deterministic enforcement — it checks every file write regardless of Claude's behavior. For a 'must always' compliance requirement, deterministic enforcement is architecturally superior.
whyWrong: Automatic application doesn't compensate for probabilistic compliance — CLAUDE.md can be violated
This is the correct answer
They are NOT equivalent — CLAUDE.md is probabilistic, hooks are deterministic. Critical distinction for compliance
Latency is a real tradeoff but doesn't change the architectural correctness — for compliance requirements, determinism wins

question: A new developer joins the team, clones the repository, and opens Claude Code. Which CLAUDE.md settings does she have immediately available?
options: User-level CLAUDE.md only (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) since it's installed system-wide
Project-level and directory-level CLAUDE.md files from the repository, but NOT user-level
All three levels: user-level, project-level, and directory-level
No CLAUDE.md settings — she must configure them manually after onboarding
explanation: Project-level (.claude/CLAUDE.md) and directory-level (CLAUDE.md in subdirectories) are committed to git and available immediately after cloning. User-level (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md) is personal, NOT committed to git, and must be created manually on each developer's machine.
whyWrong: User-level CLAUDE.md is personal and not in the repo — a new developer has no user-level settings until they create them
This is the correct answer
User-level is NOT available from cloning — it must be set up manually per developer
Project-level and directory-level CLAUDE.md files are in the repo and immediately available

question: A developer asks Claude Code to 'migrate the entire authentication system from JWT to session-based auth.' Claude starts immediately modifying files. What should have happened first?
options: Claude should have asked for the developer's preferred session storage mechanism
Claude should have entered plan mode to produce an implementation plan for review before modifying any files
Claude should have run the test suite first to establish a baseline
Claude should have created a new git branch before starting
explanation: A complete authentication system migration will touch many files (likely 30+), involves multiple valid architectural approaches, and has significant risk if done incorrectly. This is a clear plan mode trigger: large scope + multiple valid approaches + high risk = create and review a plan BEFORE modifying any files.
whyWrong: Storage mechanism is a good question but comes during planning, not before deciding to plan
This is the correct answer
Running tests is a good practice but it's part of the implementation phase, not a substitute for planning
Creating a branch is good hygiene but doesn't address the core issue of unplanned execution

question: A developer asks Claude Code to fix a failing test. Claude reads the test, identifies the issue in the source code, and fixes it. Which approach is correct?
options: Plan mode should be used because modifying source code is a critical operation
Direct execution is appropriate — single file, clear scope, test defines correct behavior
Claude should ask clarifying questions before touching any production code
Claude should fix the test to match the code rather than fix the code to match the test
explanation: A failing test with a clear diagnosis is exactly when direct execution is appropriate: single file, unambiguous scope, and the test defines correct behavior. Plan mode would add unnecessary overhead. The test is the executable specification — fix the code to pass the test.
whyWrong: Plan mode is for large scope or ambiguous approaches — a single failing test fix doesn't warrant it
This is the correct answer
The scope is completely clear — there's nothing to clarify. Tests define the correct behavior.
Tests are the spec — never modify tests to match code; fix the code to match tests

question: A team uses Claude to generate code and then asks the same Claude session to review it for bugs. Reviews consistently miss the same issues the code generation introduced. What is the root cause?
options: The code review prompt needs more specific criteria for what to look for
Claude's context window is too full after code generation to perform quality review
The same session retains generation reasoning context, biasing review toward approving its own decisions
Code review requires access to the full git history which the session doesn't have
explanation: Self-review fails because Claude retains its reasoning context from code generation. It 'remembers' why it made each decision and is much less likely to question those decisions during review. The fix is a completely separate API call (independent instance) with no knowledge of the generation process.
whyWrong: Better review criteria help but don't address the fundamental bias from shared reasoning context
Context window fill can be an issue but is not the root cause of consistently missing the same introduced bugs
This is the correct answer
Git history is not required for code review — the code itself is sufficient

question: A team wants Claude Code to analyze security vulnerabilities in changed files for every pull request. The review should check only for security issues, not style or coverage. Which approach best implements this?
options: One Claude Code review step that checks all quality dimensions simultaneously
A Claude Code step with --allowedTools 'file_read,grep,glob' focused exclusively on security criteria
A Claude Code step that runs after the existing style and coverage checks complete
Add security review instructions to the project CLAUDE.md so Claude checks automatically
explanation: Specialized review stages with restricted tools and focused criteria produce better findings than generalist reviews. --allowedTools 'file_read,grep,glob' prevents file writes in a review-only step. Specific security criteria prevent scope creep. This is the correct D3+D4 specialized review pattern.
whyWrong: Checking all dimensions at once causes attention dilution — security findings get mixed with style noise
This is the correct answer
Sequencing after other checks doesn't address the specialization requirement
CLAUDE.md provides guidance but not automated CI execution — the question asks for PR automation

question: A team wants a reusable workflow for comprehensive security audits that reads many files and produces verbose intermediate output. Should this be implemented as a Slash Command or a Skill?
options: Slash Command — it's reusable and should be triggered with /security-audit
Skill with context: fork — verbose discovery runs in isolation, main context receives only the summary
Either is equivalent — both run reusable workflows
Neither — security audits should be run as standalone scripts, not through Claude Code
explanation: Skills with context: fork run in an isolated sub-session. Verbose file reading and intermediate output don't pollute the main context window. The main session receives only the structured summary. Slash Commands inject a prompt into the CURRENT session — the verbose output would fill the main context.
whyWrong: A Slash Command runs in the main session — verbose audit output would fill the context window
This is the correct answer
They are NOT equivalent — Slash Commands run in main context, Skills run in isolated sub-sessions
Claude Code is well-suited for security audits — Skills are exactly the right mechanism

question: A coordinator agent is asked to 'analyze the impact of our new API on all dependent systems.' It spawns subagents for: API contract changes, database schema changes, and frontend component updates. The synthesis is incomplete. Which anti-pattern occurred?
options: The subagents had too many tools and made wrong tool selections
The coordinator trap — the decomposition missed entire categories of dependent systems (documentation, tests, monitoring, third-party integrations)
The synthesis step didn't have access to all subagent results simultaneously
Plan mode should have been used before spawning subagents
explanation: The coordinator trap: the coordinator decomposed 'all dependent systems' into only three categories (API contracts, DB schema, frontend) and missed entire categories of dependent systems — documentation, tests, monitoring integrations, third-party API consumers, CI/CD pipelines. The subagents succeeded at their assigned tasks but the decomposition was incomplete.
whyWrong: Tool selection issues would cause subagent execution failures, not a missing category problem
This is the correct answer
Synthesis access to results is a technical issue — the problem here is conceptual: wrong decomposition
Plan mode helps with execution sequencing, not with ensuring comprehensive scope enumeration

question: A developer wants to add personal API keys and machine-specific environment variables to their Claude Code configuration. Where should this go?
options: Project-level .claude/CLAUDE.md so it's available to the whole team
User-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md — personal config not committed to git
A directory-level CLAUDE.md in the project root
The .env file committed to the repository
explanation: Personal configuration (API keys, machine-specific paths, individual preferences) belongs in user-level ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. This file is NOT committed to git — it's personal to the developer's machine. Putting credentials in project-level CLAUDE.md would expose them to the entire team via git.
whyWrong: Project-level CLAUDE.md is committed to git — committing API keys here is a serious security issue
This is the correct answer
Directory-level CLAUDE.md is committed to git and shared with the team — not for personal config
Committing .env files with real credentials to git is a critical security anti-pattern

question: A long-running Claude Code session has been analyzing a large codebase for 2 hours. The developer notices responses are getting slower and less accurate. What is the most likely cause and correct action?
options: The model is experiencing fatigue — restart with a fresh API connection
The context window is filling up with accumulated tool results and history — use /compact to compress
The session needs to be resumed with --resume to restore performance
Increase max_tokens to give Claude more room to reason
explanation: Long sessions accumulate tool results (file reads, grep outputs) that fill the context window. When context is near-full, the lost-in-the-middle effect degrades quality. /compact compresses conversation history while preserving key findings, restoring effective context space without losing session state.
whyWrong: LLMs don't experience fatigue — the issue is context window management, not model state
This is the correct answer
--resume is for resuming a paused session — the session is currently active
max_tokens controls response length, not the context window for processing — this doesn't solve the problem

question: A team's Claude Code review integration is flagging 40 issues per PR. Developers have stopped reading the reviews. What is the most likely root cause and correct fix?
options: The review runs too slowly — optimize for speed to reduce developer friction
Too many review stages — consolidate into one comprehensive review
Criteria are too vague (e.g., 'find issues'), causing over-flagging and false positives that erode developer trust
Developers need better training on how to interpret Claude Code reviews
explanation: When developers stop reading reviews, the signal-to-noise ratio is too low — too many false positives. Vague criteria like 'find code quality issues' cause Claude to flag preferences as problems. The fix is explicit, verifiable criteria with clear scope exclusions. Specific criteria → fewer, higher-signal findings → developer trust.
whyWrong: Speed is not causing developers to ignore reviews — content quality is the issue
Consolidating into one stage increases attention dilution and makes the noise problem worse
This is the correct answer
Developer training doesn't fix a system that produces too many false positives

question: You want to enforce API route conventions only for files in src/api/ but NOT for test files in src/api/__tests__/. Which mechanism handles this correctly?
options: A directory-level CLAUDE.md in src/api/ — it applies to all files in the directory
A path-specific rule with paths: ['src/api/**/*.ts', '!src/api/**/*.test.ts']
A project-level CLAUDE.md section that specifies the API conventions
Two separate CLAUDE.md files — one in src/api/ and one in src/api/__tests__/
explanation: Path-specific rules with glob patterns and exclusions (!) are the correct mechanism for this precision targeting. A directory CLAUDE.md would apply to ALL files in src/api/ including tests. The exclusion pattern '!src/api/**/*.test.ts' precisely excludes test files from the API conventions.
whyWrong: Directory CLAUDE.md applies to everything in the directory — can't exclude test files
This is the correct answer
Project-level CLAUDE.md applies globally — can't be scoped to specific directories
Two CLAUDE.md files don't solve the problem — the api/ one still applies to __tests__/

question: An agent loop runs for exactly 10 iterations on every task, regardless of complexity. Simple tasks that complete in 3 steps still run 10 iterations. What is the anti-pattern?
options: The agent is using too many tools per loop iteration
An iteration counter is being used as the PRIMARY stopping mechanism instead of stop_reason
The agent's system prompt is not specific enough about when to stop
The max_tokens parameter is too low, causing repeated truncation
explanation: The loop is terminating on an iteration counter (max_iterations=10) rather than on stop_reason == 'end_turn'. Iteration caps should be safety valves only — if Claude signals completion via stop_reason == 'end_turn' at iteration 3, the loop should return. Using iteration count as primary termination is a fundamental anti-pattern.
whyWrong: Tool count per iteration doesn't cause exactly 10 iterations every time
This is the correct answer
The system prompt can't cause exactly 10 iterations — stop_reason is what drives the loop
max_tokens truncation causes stop_reason == 'max_tokens', not exactly 10 iterations

question: A developer asks Claude Code to implement a complex feature with several design decisions. Claude immediately starts writing code. What should Claude do instead?
options: Create a design document first, then implement
Use the interview pattern — list all questions and ambiguities before writing any code
Ask one question at a time in a back-and-forth dialogue
Run plan mode and proceed without developer input
explanation: The interview pattern: Claude lists ALL questions and ambiguities at once, developer answers all at once, then implementation proceeds without interruption. This surfaces implementation decisions (format, scale, security, edge cases) before code is written — preventing mid-implementation backtracking. One-question-at-a-time dialogue is slow and breaks flow.
whyWrong: Design documents are output artifacts — the interview pattern produces the inputs that would feed a design document
This is the correct answer
One question at a time is slow and interrupts the developer's workflow repeatedly
Plan mode without input misses the ambiguities that the developer needs to resolve first

question: A coordinator agent spawns 3 code analysis subagents in parallel. Subagent 2 fails with a permission error accessing a private repository. What should subagent 2 return and what should the coordinator do?
options: Subagent 2 returns an empty result; coordinator continues with results from 1 and 3
Subagent 2 returns a structured error with error_category='permission' and is_retryable=False; coordinator synthesizes from 1 and 3 and explicitly notes the missing analysis
Subagent 2 retries with elevated permissions automatically
The coordinator aborts the entire task since all subagents must succeed
explanation: Subagent 2 returns a structured error (not empty — silent failure is the anti-pattern) with error_category='permission' and is_retryable=False. The coordinator uses the structured error to decide: don't retry (it's a permission error), synthesize from available results (1 and 3), and explicitly note the missing private repository analysis in the output.
whyWrong: Empty result is silent failure — coordinator cannot distinguish it from 'no findings' and makes uninformed decisions
This is the correct answer
Subagents don't autonomously escalate permissions — they report errors to the coordinator which decides
Aborting on any failure wastes the successful work from subagents 1 and 3 — partial success is valid

question: A slash command needs to accept a file path as input. Which mechanism allows this in Claude Code?
options: The command automatically receives the currently open file
The $ARGUMENTS placeholder in the command file captures text typed after the command name
A separate config file specifies which arguments the command accepts
Arguments are passed via environment variables before running the command
explanation: $ARGUMENTS is the placeholder in slash command markdown files that captures everything the user types after the command name. For example: '/review-pr src/api/payments.ts' — the $ARGUMENTS would contain 'src/api/payments.ts' and the command file can use it in its prompt.
whyWrong: Commands don't automatically receive the open file — the developer must specify what to pass
This is the correct answer
No separate argument config file exists — $ARGUMENTS in the command file handles this
Environment variables are for configuration, not for dynamic arguments passed at invocation time

question: Which scenario correctly describes when prompt guidance is sufficient vs when programmatic enforcement is required?
options: Prompt guidance for tone and style; programmatic enforcement for financial transaction limits
Prompt guidance for everything — sufficiently detailed prompts achieve the same compliance as code
Programmatic enforcement for everything — prompts are too unreliable for production use
Prompt guidance for new features; programmatic enforcement only for legacy system integrations
explanation: The decision rule: tone, style, and soft preferences → prompt guidance (occasional deviation is acceptable). Financial limits, compliance requirements, security controls → programmatic enforcement (deterministic, 0% failure rate required). The distinction is whether a failure has material consequences.
whyWrong: This is the correct answer
Prompts are probabilistic — for 'must always' compliance requirements, probabilistic is insufficient
Programmatic enforcement for everything is excessive — prompts are perfectly adequate for preferences and style
The age of a feature doesn't determine the enforcement mechanism — the consequence of failure does
